title: Preventing and spotting mastitis in a meat doe
subtitle: The infection that takes a nursing doe and her kittens together if it is missed.
excerpt: Mastitis in a nursing meat doe shows up as a hot, swollen, painful section of the udder, sometimes with a discharge. Caught on the first day it is treatable; caught on day three the doe is septic. A walk through hutch husbandry that prevents most cases, the daily check that catches the rest, and the early treatment that keeps the litter alive.
sourceNotes: American Rabbit Breeders Association husbandry notes. House Rabbit Society veterinary health pages. UK Rabbit Council, breeder welfare guidance. SAC Consulting Scotland, small-livestock health notes (rabbit section).

glossaryTerms.definition: Verb: to give birth, in rabbits. A doe kindles her litter on a 31 to 33 day cycle from mating.
The wooden or wire-and-cardboard box fitted in the hutch a few days before kindling, where the doe builds her fur-lined nest and the litter spends the first three weeks.
Newborn rabbits, from kindling to weaning around 6 to 8 weeks. A doe with a meat-litter typically raises 6 to 10 kits per litter.

content.text: A meat doe with mastitis can be saved if the keeper sees the swelling on the first day. By the second day she is off her food and the litter is suckling on a poisoned udder. By the third day she is septic and the kits are dying with her. There is no margin in this one.

The good news is that the conditions that produce mastitis are the easy ones to fix. A clean, dry, fly-free nest box in a hutch with good ventilation and a calm doe carries very few cases through to weaning. Most working meat operations on a small scale do not see mastitis from one year to the next once the husbandry is right.
What mastitis looks like

The doe sits in a corner of the hutch with her belly raised slightly off the floor and her ears flat. A look at the underside shows one or more teats hot, swollen, and dark pink or bluish. The skin around the affected gland is tight. There may be a thin discharge from the teat when gently pressed. The doe will flinch from the contact.

An advanced case has the affected gland visibly purple-black (this is the old term, blue bag, that you may still see in older books). The doe will be off her food, refusing to drink, with her temperature high enough to feel through the ear base. At this stage the case is past home treatment and the vet decision is whether to put her down to stop the suffering and pull the litter for hand-rearing.
Hutch husbandry that prevents most cases
A dry, clean nest box, refreshed the day before the doe is due to kindle. Damp bedding causes most early infections.
A hutch floor that drains well. Wet patches under a nursing doe trap bacteria and flies.
A doe in good condition before kindling. Underweight does carry less reserve and recover slowly from any infection.

Litter sizes the doe can support. A doe with 12 kits and 8 teats is at higher risk than one with 8 kits and 8 teats; the over-suckling of some teats and the abandoning of others both trigger inflammation. Foster some kits to a second doe if you have one available.
Good ventilation without a draught on the doe. Stagnant air and ammonia both wear at the udder skin and make it easier for bacteria to enter.
Fly control in summer: a fine mesh over the hutch opening if flies are bad, and dropping-tray clean-out twice weekly so flies have nowhere to breed.
The daily udder check during lactation
From the day after
kindle

until weaning, the doe gets a once-a-day look at the underside. Lift her gently out of the hutch and onto a clean board with her back end towards you. Part the belly fur. Each of the eight to ten teats should look pink, soft, and warm but not hot. A small bead of milk at any of the teats is normal during peak lactation. Anything hot, hard, very pale, or very dark is the early warning sign.

attrs.title: What a healthy lactating udder feels like
attrs.body: Soft, even warmth across all teats. Slight give under finger pressure. No flinch from the doe. Bead of clear-white milk at gentle pressure. Skin colour pink along the belly line. Variation between teats is fine (the front teats often produce more); what matters is whether one gland is markedly different from the others on the same doe.

content.text: Treatment when caught early
On day one of a hot teat, ring the vet. Move the doe and her
nest box

into a clean hutch. Apply warm compresses to the affected gland four times a day. The vet will prescribe a course of antibiotic suitable for rabbits. Not all antibiotics are safe; penicillin and amoxicillin can be fatal in rabbits. Leave the choice to the vet.
Keep the
kits

on the doe if she will let them feed and if the affected gland is only one or two of the teats. If the infection has spread to most of the udder, hand-rearing or fostering on a second doe is the safer route. A vet-supervised case caught on day one has a high recovery rate.
When prevention has failed

If a doe is lost to mastitis or comes close to it, take a careful look at the conditions before the next breeding. A wet patch in the corner of the hutch, a draught from the open mesh wall, a litter that was too large, a feed change made too close to kindling: these are the usual causes and each is something to change for the next round. Strip the hutch out to bare wood, scrub with a veterinary disinfectant, leave to dry in sunlight for a week before any other doe goes in.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Common udder findings during the daily check
attrs.intro: What different presentations point to and what to do.

items.symptom: One teat slightly warmer than the others on day one of lactation.
items.cause: Hot teat is the early sign; could be the first hour of mastitis or just heavy feeding.
items.fix: Re-check in four hours. If still hot, treat as mastitis day one: vet call, warm compresses, move to clean hutch.

items.symptom: Hard lump in one gland, doe still feeding the kits normally.
items.cause: Mid-stage mastitis or a milk-flow blockage.
items.fix: Vet call. The hard lump means the gland is shutting down; antibiotic plus careful manual drainage of the affected teat may save the doe even at this stage.

items.symptom: Doe off feed for 24 hours, udder hot and dark.
items.cause: Established mastitis, often with septicaemia setting in.
items.fix: Vet call urgently. The litter may need pulling for hand-rearing or fostering whatever the outcome for the doe.

items.symptom: Multiple does on the holding with mastitis in the same season.
items.cause: Husbandry issue across the rabbitry, not a one-off.
items.fix: Audit hutch dryness, ventilation, fly control, feed mix, and breeding age. Most multi-case rabbitries find the trigger in damp floors or poor airflow.

items.symptom: Doe makes it through but the affected teat stays small at the next lactation.
items.cause: The gland has fibrosed; that teat will not produce again.
items.fix: Manage the next litter size to the working teats. A doe who has lost a teat to mastitis is still useful; her milking capacity is just slightly reduced.

content.text: What to write in the rabbitry book

Date, doe ear-tag number, what the udder looked like, what was done. Across breedings a pattern often emerges: a particular doe is prone, a particular hutch has more problems than the others, or a particular season is worse. The notes are what turn one incident into the change that prevents the next.
